segments.text: The first way is to improve embeddings with existing Lexicons.
And this example is using non-contextual embeddings,
like not the ones we get from neural language models,
but once we get from just running a embedding model
like WordDevac or something like this.
And what they did in this paper is they essentially
retrofitted embeddings to existing Lexicons
by doing post-hoc transformations of the embeddings
so that they matched the knowledge graph or Lexicon better.
And so the way they did this is they started out
with pre-trained embeddings.
And they had a double objective of making
the transform embeddings close to the neighbors
and close to the original embedding.
And the way they did this is they essentially
had this regularization term over here.
So this regularization term is basically saying,
I don't want you to move your embeddings too far away
from how they were initialized.
And then at the same time, I would like you to make
these embeddings closer to each other
if they are synonyms of each other.
So they did this using WordNet
and they basically took the words that were synonyms
to each other in syn sets with each other.
And they tried to regularize the synonyms
to be closer together, but also the embeddings
to be closer to how they started out.
And there were also examples of forcing
antonyms away from each other.
So like if you're, this is a little bit of an older work.
So it was working on non-contextualized embeddings,
but we could do something very similar
for more modern models and like knowledge graph embeddings,
for example.

and then different examples of those entities
across different contexts.

like Joe Biden, Joseph Biden, Joseph Arbiden,

of things that match these strings
and even do entity linking,

And then encourage the embeddings
for all of these different instances to be closer together,
to make your model like distinguish them less
and ensure that they get closer embeddings
and they could improve like question answering,
look up, other stuff like that.

the embedding from the beginning of the span,
the embedding from the end of the span.

like a neural network and get a new embedding.
